confabulation, right? And just good enough to sort of serve the needs of satisfying or convincing others. You have other work that's about not being gullible, like not born yesterday, that people hold good.
maybe beliefs is the oversimplified version of that. I'm sort of wondering if you can connect what we've just been talking about now to this other idea of yours that I think has been controversial and you can maybe talk more about this or maybe even more clearly what the claim is because

and are sort of just will -o-wisp maybe not very good. How do you square that distinction with this other body of work that's about people are really good at holding beliefs and whole beliefs that are sort of good for them? Yeah, that's a great question. So the points I've been making in that book and that
also kind of done and a bunch of colleagues have been making in the paper called Epistemic Vigilance is that people
have to have mechanisms to evaluate communicated information because we can't just take each other's word for it because we have conflicting or to some extent we have non-perfectly overlapping incentives and interests so even when people you know close close to you tell you something you can't just accept that it's going to be in your best interest to accept it
And so we have to have cognitive mechanisms that allow us to evaluate what other people are telling us and what they're communicating to us. And I think that really applies as we're saying, and that's why I was pushing back a bit at the beginning on David's point that people can just make stuff up. So people can make stuff up, but other people, if they...
disagree with in the first place they will evaluate that critically and the exception to that in a way is when you agree with the conclusion that is that people want to have a confabulation or justification for and then you're happy to have one as well
And so, you know, if you're one of the people who is happy to have a scapegoat who will be accused of, you know, making someone suffer a misfortune, you're happy to have this justification that actually, you know, that guy is a witch and to have this whole belief system. So you become much more, I mean, credible is the wrong term because it's kind of smart of you to accept these things, but you become more kind of laxer in your.
when you're criteria when you already accept the conclusion and it's true for you in general if you if you accept a given conclusion you know you don't really care about the arguments because the arguments are not going to make you change your mind and they're just there that you could potentially reuse to try to convince other people so
So these two things are quite coherent, but it's also important to stress that when you do disagree, then you'll be much more critical. And so, yeah, I mean, when you're the ethnographer and you, you know, especially in earlier periods and you arrive in these people and they believe in witches like, well, no, obviously there are no witches, you know, all of a sudden convinced that there are witches. So, you know, these justifications only convince the people who have a motivation to believe in them in the first place.
Yeah. And then the disagreement or so that argument has this claim of, you know, the not born yesterday idea or the epistemic vigilance. Has it ever has it been misunderstood? Do people think you're making a broader claim or in what way has it been misunderstood or taken too far? Yes, I mean, I'm not sure because I'm taking it far enough. I'm not sure how many people have tried to take it further. Yeah. But yeah, I mean, I don't mean to say that.
people are never wrong because obviously there are sometimes. The point I'm trying to make is that when people are wrong usually it's not because of communication, usually it's because their priors are wrong. So if you ask people you know if you go to a small scale society and you ask them what is the shape of the earth and maybe they're gonna mostly they're not gonna know but you know maybe they're gonna tell you some weird narrative or that it's flat essentially because that's what they're saying.
It's not because they've been brainwashed into thinking that it's just because it's either what their senses are telling them or because they have a story that allows them to justify something that's important to them. So it's not people are you know they have obviously they are wrong about some things but it's rarely it's rare that mass communication or communication more generally is the cause.
of these mistaken beliefs like it can or I mean to take another example like you know
Obviously if you grew up in a specific small scale society and you have really specific religious beliefs and you believe that this ancestor does that to these people for that reason, this is heavily shaped by communication because you know the name of the ancestor or the specific form that they take is specific to your culture. So in the same way as when you grew up speaking English it's because you had you know English speaking peers and parents. But the fact that everybody speaks a language is you know the fact that everybody in small scale society.
in other societies believes in these kind of supernatural entities, it suggests that even if the specific details of any one of them is due to communication, the broad form is there because people are pre-wired to believe in this sort of thing. So their priors for this sort of things existing or their incentives for believing in this sort of things are very high to start with and the culture gives you the specifics rather than the more fundamental idea.
Hmm. Interesting.
So I want to, so there's a wonderful key distinction you make in Not Born Yesterday that helps you make sense of our epistemic vigilance or our skepticism toward communicated information on the one hand and the seemingly wide variety of wacky beliefs that humans seem to fall prey to. And the distinction you make is between intuitive beliefs and reflective beliefs. So intuitive beliefs are the ones that...
are often they actually guide our behavior in a real concrete sense so I intuitively believe that there's a coffee cup on the table so I'm going to reach in that direction to pick up the coffee cup my belief about where the coffee cup is is directly connected to my nervous system such that I reach in a particular direction and that's an intuitive belief
And then there's this different category called reflective belief that doesn't really guide my behavior in the same way. It's kind of functionally inert. And your argument, you can correct me if I'm wrong, is that a lot of these seemingly wacky beliefs are not actually intuitive beliefs. They're not actually guiding people's behavior in a concrete way. They're just kind of reflective beliefs that they're serving.
social functions and people don't really believe them deep down in a way that is actually guiding their behavior. So could you flesh out this idea of, you know, what this difference is between intuitive and reflective, why we would, why the mind should be divided up in this way, and how we figure out, you know, whether to adopt something as an intuitive belief versus a reflective belief.
Thanks, that's a great question. The first thing I'd say is all of that comes from Dan Acton. He has a wonderful paper called Intuitive and Reflexive Beliefs. All of this framework is really kind of his ideas, Dan's forever.
So an illustration I like is the contrast between people who have actual experiences of conspiracies and conspiracy theorists, people who believe in conspiracy theories, and everything we know about people who have actually witnessed a conspiracy. So you've seen your boss shredding documents that are very compromising, you've seen someone in your company do something bad, you've seen someone in government do something extremely fishy, and the behavior of these people is consistently to be afraid.
to it's really hard to tell people who would be kind of whistleblowers to reveal their secrets you know they're afraid for their jobs and if you're in a bad country you can be afraid for your life you're afraid you know to jeopardize your social relationships if you say anything and so most people's reactions is to be first of all to be really scared it's very strong emotionally
And then it's really hard, you don't say anything as a rule. And that's the exact opposite of what most people who endorse conspiracy theories feel. Like they're not, even if they believe that the CIA is powerful enough to pull off 9-11, they're not afraid they're going to say that in public, which is quite inconsistent in the sense that if the CIA was powerful enough to pull off 9-11, they could kill you quite easily. So you wouldn't want to actually say that.
So they don't have this, you know, they have some emotional reactions to these conspiracies, but they're really muted compared to someone who would actually, who has seen actual evidence.
But by contrast, they are very vocal, like a lot of them are really very vocal, which is then again the opposite of what you see with someone who has formed an intuitive belief because they've seen actual evidence. So you have really these two causal pathways. One is you actually you see actual evidence, you form an intuitive belief that someone in your company is a crook or that there's more widespread corruption in your company or in your government and your reactions are really strong emotionally and you're being very careful.
versus someone who has
heard of something and they're happy to profess these views because it allows them to justify their mistrust of government or for some other reasons or they can kind of score points to with people who are similarly inclined and you have these really two things that are completely different in interactions and so the reason why we have intuitive beliefs is obvious is because you know they guide our behavior in the world that's how we we know we can act adaptively and the reason why we were able to hold reflective beliefs is
This is more subtle, obviously.
The basic cognitive mechanisms that allow us to have beliefs that are encapsulated, that are detached from the rest of our cognition, is that we have to have that to entertain the idea that other people can be wrong. So if you tell me something and I think you're wrong, I have to be able to think, well, you know, David thinks that without that thought, affecting at all my behavior directly. So you can affect my behavior in the sense I can tell you you're wrong or I can...
and action aware that takes into account the fact that I think you have that belief but you know if you think a given restaurant is great but I think you're completely wrong I'm not just going to go there because now I think it's great so we have to have that ability to separate some beliefs from the rest
And what's tricky with reflective beliefs is that on the one hand they are held in that little box in which we usually keep things that we think are false, but the official attitude we have towards these beliefs is that we believe them. So we know it's cognitively possible to have things that are insulated because it has to be the case for us to be able to hold, to think that other people think things that are false, or that we think are false.
And sometimes it just so happens that these beliefs can stay there even though we supposedly have reasons to believe that they're true. And it's not completely clear why they stay there. So Dan's examples typically are things that we can't understand. So if a child who accepts that something about the Holy Trinity is mine just
You can't have an intuitive belief about the Holy Trinity because your mind doesn't know what to do with it. It's just not something that we evolved to deal with. But something like a conspiracy theory, your mind knows what to do. Like if you had actual evidence that when you have actual evidence of a conspiracy, your mind knows exactly what to do with that information. So it's not entirely clear why it's possible both for you to think you have enough evidence to...
to accept it, that you don't think it's false, but at the same time to not act on that basis. And I don't really know actually, to be honest, why that happens. Interesting. So, I mean, the way I'm starting to think of it is that we draw all sorts of inferences about people based on their actions. And one category that we are eager to draw inferences about people.
Toward is the category of belief. So what you believe gives me a lot of inferences about your character, about your personality, your loyalties, your commitments, etc. And what I've written about myself is that as soon as you have a Q-based inference where everyone in a society is drawing an inference, if X then Y, well that creates a social incentive for everyone to transform that Q into a signal. So if I start drawing the inference about you.
you, that if you believe that fetuses are people, therefore you are a loyal Republican, well, that gives you an incentive to conspicuously signal that you believe fetuses are people to get me to think that you're a committed Republican, right? So as soon as you, if you have a queue-based inference set up, that creates an incentive for it to turn into a signal or an intentional signal.
And so I think what's going on with a lot of these reflective beliefs is it's just what happens when everyone draws the same inference, cue-based inferences about a particular belief, and then all of a sudden it transforms into a signal. So if you believe in the Holy Trinity, you're a loyal member of the group, as soon as we all make that inference, it creates an incentive for everyone to try to conspicuously signal that they believe in the Holy Trinity. And in that case, it's not necessarily...
um a an intuitive belief it's it's a social signal it's you could think of it as a pseudo belief i'm trying to mimic the superficial properties of belief in order to get you to think certain things about me to think that i'm a loyal christian you could think of it as more of a social strategy than a belief per se
And so I think one way that these kind of reflective beliefs can come about is through kind of social signaling mechanisms. And it's a pseudo belief in the same way that pseudoscience is a mimicry of science. It's the same, it arises in the same way as any other kind of pseudo process where there are benefits to be had by mimicking a certain thing. And this happens in belief just as it happens in a wide variety of cases in our culture. What do you think about that way of thinking about it?
Yep. Yeah, no, no, I think it's part of the answer in many cases. But so for something to become culturally widespread, you have to have some motivation to share it and that's what you're describing. But people still have to have some, like at least at the beginning to kick off the process, people have to have some motivation for accepting it as well. And so...
The mechanism still has to pass at least a minimum threshold of, you know, epistemic vigilance. And so, you know, conspiracy theories are, I mean.
The ones that are the most popular are the ones that are somewhat credible like you know a lot of people believe Kennedy there was something fishy and you know even if it turns out that it's ultimately false you can see why that would be kind of you know broadly plausible for many reasons whereas the people who hold the ones that you know that they thought the Queen of England was a lizard person these are people who these are vastly less popular.
and they only spread in an audience of people who already believe things are a little bit crazy from most people so so you still need to explain so even if if there is this this signaling component for the thing to become more spread and to really entirely then then be able to fulfill its signaling component people still need to accept it in some way like you know you couldn't
If you take a population that has kind of normal priors, you can't just decide, well, now we're all going to signal that we believe the moon is made of cheese because the thing is not going to spread in the first place. So you still have to have some reason why people are accepting the thing at some point. Yeah, no, absolutely. Yeah, there needs to be some way of keeping the signal honest.
And it could be that only people who are actually committed to the group are willing to send the signal because that in effect alienates you from outgroups. So if everyone in our group believes in the Trinity, everyone in the outgroup rejects that belief, merely by uttering that belief becomes a kind of costly signal because I'm burning bridges with the outgroup. And so that could be one way of maintaining signal honesty. Another way is maybe I guess.
Maybe I pay some cost. So if people who believe in Jesus go to these rituals and make these sacrifices, maybe I perform those rituals and make those sacrifices. Even if I don't truly believe in an intuitive level in the particular God or spirit, if I'm making the sacrifices, that could maintain the honesty of the signal. And in other cases, maybe it is just a deceptive signal.
just in the same way that pseudoscience is a deceptive signal it's not really science but just people just you know get get fooled it happens and you know maybe it'll the the the trickery will will persist for some amount of time maybe at some point people will discover it and the pseudoscientist will his reputation will collapse and then a new pseudoscience will emerge that tries to mimic some other superficial property of science and you get this kind of arms race between deceivers and deceived
So that's another path that these kinds of pseudo-beliefs could take. But yeah, that's sort of how I'm thinking of it, that either it remains a deceptive signal, in which case you get a kind of arms race and sometimes it persists and sometimes it doesn't, or it has a kind of bridge-burning function or it takes on some kind of cost that makes the signal persist. Okay, none of these are interesting possibilities that must apply in some cases. Like I'm just saying that the...
I guess the point I was trying to make is that it seems as if what you're saying already takes for granted that the belief is widespread enough that it can be used as a reliable signal.
